summary: Jeff Atwood explores the surprising efficiency of paper as a data storage medium by comparing various visual encoding methods, from punch cards to QR codes to printed text. He introduces PaperBack, software that can store up to 500KB on a single printed page using bitmap encoding. The post argues that while digital storage formats constantly evolve and become obsolete, paper has proven archival longevity measured in centuries. Atwood makes the case that for critical data like cryptographic keys, combining digital encoding with physical paper offers unique advantages in durability and human-readability that modern storage media cannot match.
key_points: The alphabet itself is a form of visual encoding that we process automatically, but it's not the most efficient way to store data on paper
A single sheet of paper can store up to 500,000 bytes using bitmap encoding versus roughly 10,000 characters of printed text
Paper has superior archival longevity compared to digital media - lasting hundreds of years versus the uncertain lifespan of CDs, tapes, and hard drives
The storage industry is littered with obsolete formats, making long-term data retrieval uncertain even when physical media survives
For critical backups like cryptographic keys, paper serves as a reliable last-resort recovery method
money_quotes: The alphabet is no different than any other optical machine readable input, except the machines are us.
Paper the way we typically use it is criminally inefficient. It has a ton of wasted data storage space.
By looking on CD or magnetic tape, you are not able to tell whether your data is readable or not.

For paper, on the other hand, to claim it will last for 100 years is not even vaguely impressive. High-quality paper with good ink regularly lasts many hundreds of years even under less than optimal conditions.
The storage industry is littered with old now-dead ways of storing data.
Computer encoding formats and data storage schemes come and go.
A combination of simple digital encoding and the good old boring, reliable, really really old school technology of physical paper can still make sense.
key_insight: Despite constant technological advancement in digital storage, paper remains a uniquely reliable archival medium because its longevity is measured in centuries and its readability doesn't depend on having compatible hardware.
